aliases: Juice drink with mixed fruit concentrate

english: Juice drink with mixed fruit concentrate

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for mixed fruit juice drink; SNOMED CT coverage for processed beverage subcategories is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is one glass of approximately 250 ml, equivalent to 250 g, as commonly consumed as a snack or mealtime beverage.

nutrient_source.title: Mixed Fruit Juice Drink — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for mixed fruit juice drink (cr_id D03070022). All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — fruit juice and juice drink category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 55 is considered 'low' according to the University of Sydney GI Database classification (GI < 55 is low, 55-69 is medium, > 70 is high). The input record GI value of 55 and GL of 5.3 per 100 g (= 55 × 9.55 / 100 ≈ 5.25, consistent) are preserved; GL per serving scaled to 250 g portion. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 23.875) / 100 ≈ 13.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.3 g/100 g) in a sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk via insulin-driven sodium retention, despite low absolute sodium (11 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this sugar-sweetened beverage, as its 9.3 g/100 g sugar content exceeds the AHA 2018 beverage sugar threshold of 5 g/100 g and independently raises blood pressure risk through insulin-mediated sodium retention; replace with plain water or unsweetened beverages.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Liquid sugar from fruit juice concentrate (9.3 g sugar/100 g, GI 55) is rapidly absorbed without the fibre buffer of whole fruit, causing significant postprandial glucose excursions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage; the combination of a low GI (55), high sugar load (9.3 g/100 g), and minimal dietary fibre (0.43 g/100 g) produces rapid postprandial glucose spikes — choose whole fruit with intact fibre instead, or limit intake to no more than 100 ml paired with a high-protein, high-fibre meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with elevated triglycerides, increased visceral adiposity, and heightened cardiovascular risk independent of total caloric intake.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should minimise intake of this sugar-sweetened juice drink, as habitual consumption of beverages with ≥9 g sugar/100 g is linked to elevated serum triglycerides and increased CVD risk per AHA dietary guidelines; substitute with plain water, sparkling water, or whole fruit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (9.3 g/100 g) in a processed beverage promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine release; the processed juice form lacks the whole-food polyphenol and fibre matrix of whole fruit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should choose whole fresh fruit over this processed juice drink, as the added sugar content can trigger inflammatory cytokine release and the processing removes the anti-inflammatory polyphenol and fibre matrix present in whole fruit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mixed Fruit Juice Drink is a processed, sweetened beverage made from mixed fruit juice concentrate diluted with water and sweeteners. It is not a whole-fruit juice but a juice drink, meaning it contains a lower proportion of actual fruit juice supplemented with added sugars or sweeteners to enhance palatability. The drink is typically clear to lightly coloured, with a sweet, fruity flavour profile reminiscent of tropical or mixed orchard fruits. It is consumed chilled as a snack beverage or mealtime accompaniment and is widely available in Singapore and across Southeast Asia in carton, bottle, and pouch formats. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 42 kcal, 9.55 g carbohydrate (of which 9.3 g is sugar), 0.31 g protein, 0.17 g fat, 0.43 g dietary fibre, 11 mg sodium, 26 mg calcium, and 0.56 mg iron. The high sugar content relative to fibre means glucose from this beverage is absorbed rapidly, producing a low glycemic index of approximately 55 and a glycemic load of approximately 13 per 250 ml serving. Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its sugar-sweetened beverage classification. It is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, and is classified as a processed food product. Whole fruit is consistently preferred over this product for health-conscious consumers due to the intact fibre, polyphenol, and micronutrient matrix of unprocessed fruit.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id D03070022) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and provenance is canonical. GI value of 55 is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 AJCN international GI tables for mixed fruit juice drinks.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB source record): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this product and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for a directly matching mixed fruit juice drink formulation. Calcium (26 mg/100 g) and iron (0.56 mg/100 g) are reported by HPB and retained. Glycemic load per serving recalculated for a 250 g serving: GL = (55 × 9.55 × 250/100) / 100 ≈ 13.1. The input record GL of 5.3 represents GL per 100 g (= 55 × 9.55 / 100 ≈ 5.25), which is consistent. This food is a generic beverage with no specific cuisine or regional origin requiring mandatory multilingual naming; local_names fields are null and G6 multilingual compliance is not triggered. Ontology: FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 (fruit juice drink) is a defensible exact match; FoodEx2 code A0EJW (Fruit nectars and drinks) is a defensible exact match per EFSA FoodEx2 revision 2 catalogue.
